title: The Problem
story_beat: Pink Bear wakes up full of Easter excitement, only to discover a blanket of unexpected snow has ruined all her outdoor plans.

techniques.name: show_dont_tell
techniques.reason: To vividly portray the unexpected snow and Pink Bear's initial reaction without simply stating her feelings.

techniques.name: character_voice
techniques.reason: To establish Pink Bear's unique optimistic perspective and how it shapes her experience of the problem.

techniques.name: sensory_detail
techniques.reason: To make the surprising snowy Easter morning come alive for the reader through sights, sounds, and textures.

wonder_prompt: Imagine you're Pink Bear, waking up to a surprise. How can you make your reader *feel* her excitement, then her sudden disappointment, without ever saying 'Pink Bear was excited' or 'Pink Bear was disappointed'?
wonder_example: When Sophie gets angry, really, really angry, she roars a red roar, stomps a stompy stomp, and kicks a kicky kick. She wants to smash the whole world to smithereens. (Molly Bang, When Sophie Gets Angry - Really, Really Angry...)
build_instructions: This session, focus on showing Pink Bear's initial joy and then her discovery of the snowy problem, making sure her optimistic spirit still shines through as the story engine starts.
page_prompts: Write Pink Bear's joyful awakening on Easter morning, describing her anticipation and the bright, hopeful atmosphere in Susie's room before she looks outside.
Now, write the exact moment Pink Bear first peeks out the window and discovers the unexpected blanket of snow covering Susie's garden. How does her happy expression change?
Pink Bear realizes her perfect outdoor Easter plans are ruined. Show her initial disappointment and how her optimistic nature begins to spark a tiny idea, even amidst the snow.
reflect_preview: Pink Bear has a snowy problem and a tiny spark of an idea. What will her optimistic spirit inspire her to do next to save Easter?
